summary:
_đź¤– This is an automated review. Addressing it doesn't guarantee a merge._

Replaces method swizzling on `UIWindow.gestureRecognizers` with a `UIWindow.didBecomeKeyNotification`-based approach for attaching the dev menu's three-finger long-press gesture recognizer. The old swizzling approach interfered with UIKit's internal gesture handling during `fullScreenModal` dismissals, causing app freezes (#42777). The new approach is cleaner and avoids modifying UIKit internals.

The design is sound -- one bug in the CHANGELOG and one thread-safety concern to address.

comments.body:
The link references PR #44039 which is an unrelated, already-merged CLI fix. This should reference #44096 (this PR).

```suggestion
- [iOS] Fix app freeze when dismissing a `fullScreenModal` by replacing `UIWindow.gestureRecognizers` swizzling with a notification-based approach for attaching the dev menu touch gesture recognizer. ([#44096](https://github.com/expo/expo/pull/44096) by [@WolffM](https://github.com/WolffM))
```

When `isInstalled` is set from a background thread, the actual work is dispatched asynchronously to the main thread. Since `willSet` fires before the stored property changes, this creates a window where:

1. Thread B sets `isInstalled = true` -> `willSet` dispatches async block to main thread
2. Before the block executes, the property is already `true`
3. Thread B (or another) sets `isInstalled = false` -> `willSet` fires again (old=true, new=false), dispatches another async block
4. Both blocks now execute on the main queue -- but in between, `isInstalled` has already changed

The blocks capture `newValue` so they do the right work, but the `recognizer.isEnabled = newValue` at the end of each block reads the captured value, not the current property. This means you could end up with the recognizer enabled even though `isInstalled` is `false`, if the blocks execute out of order relative to property mutations.

In practice this is unlikely because `isInstalled` is only toggled during setup and preference changes (both infrequent), but it would be more correct to always dispatch synchronously when off the main thread (like the existing `Dispatch.mainSync` helper in `DevMenuManager.swift`) or to read the current `isInstalled` value at the end of the block instead of the captured `newValue`.

Consider what happens when the app launches and no window is key yet, or when all windows are above `.normal` level (e.g. during an alert). `addRecognizerToKeyWindow()` would find no matching window and the recognizer wouldn't be attached. Then, when a normal window does become key, `windowDidBecomeKey` will fire and attach it -- so this is fine.

However, there's an edge case: if the app's main window is already key *before* the notification observer is registered (which can happen during `DevMenuPreferences.setup()` called from `DevMenuManager.init()`), you rely on `addRecognizerToKeyWindow()` to catch it. This looks correct as written -- just confirming the ordering is intentional and works.
